paragraphs.body: Main Point: through the comparison between the Downtown Pittsburgh and the Dongjiekou (东街口1) Fuzhou, I would like to illustrate the contrast of urban planning in China and the US, and highlight the point that Downtown Pittsburgh is not built for “people”, but a mere instrumental land, which I didn’t realize until I got here.

Exposition: before I came here, I thought downtown should be a welcoming, visitor- friendly place of a city, like most downtown areas I had ever been in China, or other countries. Like it is showed in the picture, what I was expecting was a place full of people, with malls (not necessarily to be shopping malls, but places that gather people) and warm lights, etc. All in all, residents and visitors could always engage in, furthermore, fit in, the vibe of the city, and obtain a sense of merging into it.

Conflict: when I first visited there, I realized: this is the definition of “Steel Forest” itself. A key feature of the building complex is that it is full of tall buildings (some are even skyscrapers, to some extent) with cold 1 Technically, there is no such a place in Fuzhou could be the immediate analogy of Downtown Pittsburgh, since the modern commercial/technological places are scattered in Fuzhou, without a concentrated area. Therefore, the reason why I chose Dongjiekou (东街口) is that it is one of the oldest commercial and population centers in Fuzhou, and usually being represented as the representative region of the city, which shows certain proximity to Downtown Pittsburgh. colors. The anticipated feeling of welcoming did not exist at all—people just walking on the street, being indifferent to everything happened around; and the most crucial difference is there was barely any interaction between pedestrians and the buildings in the city. Skyscrapers seemed to be exclusive cages expelling people from them. A sense of dividing appeared to my mind.

Rising Action: Dongjiekou indeed possesses certain common traits with Downtown Pittsburgh, e.g. fulfilled by business firms and classic buildings in the history, and so on. A significant difference between the two is the language—both cities are not so diverse from this aspect. In Dongjiekou, Chinese is used in 99% occasions, though some foreign brands would adopt certain English/French; while in Downtown Pittsburgh, English seems to be the sole language being used. Meanwhile, Dongjiekou has a way stronger interaction between visitors and the surrounding environment, all buildings are build-for-people, which presents as all gates are open to visitors. As a result, it has a warmer, more humane atmosphere than downtown in Pittsburgh.

Climax: the clear contrast indicates an essential difference between Chinese and American urban planning strategies. My opinion changed—I used to think that the commercial region is staggered with residential area in everywhere, but apparently it is not the case in the US. Obviously, the urban area and the residential area in most American cities are separate, the downtown, for example, is a pure, closed, business place, which is not constructed for living human beings, since everything is so alienated to normal people. As for residential places, they are separated from the downtown, which diverges from that of China.

Resolution: this is a very good example of broadening my horizon—a break of stereotype. It provides me a more diverse view on different urban designs around the world. It brought me to the point that on the other side of the Pacific ocean, and innovated me another possibility of modern culture.

annotations.comment: 4 The outline looks nice! You may consider using your first language in the narrative to enrich its content. Overall, good job!
